<@UHWC9PXBL> Awesome feedback :thumbsup:
Let me try this:
Here’s Alice, she’s interested in backend development. The container-world, infrastructure and devops is a little confusing…after all Alice just wants to prototype an enpoint to upload a video, ML to detect what objects are int, compress it, archive it and return a storage url. Alice discovered Storyscript. She was able to describe her business-logic in simple terms and get the job done in minutes because the tooling helped auto-complete, the services were ready-to-use like libraries and no-plumbing deployments.

(not real code, just concepts to illustrate the idea)
```
when http server listen method:"post" path:"/upload" as req
    video = req.files["upload"]
    topics = machinebox/videobox detect input:video
    res = psql insert table:"videos" data:{..stuff from above..}
    video = someOpenSourceCompressor compress video:video
    storage put content:video dest:"/{res['uuid']}.mp4"
    req write content:"/{res['uuid']}.mp4"
```

<@UHWC9PXBL> Awesome feedback :thumbsup:
Let me try this:
Here’s Alice, she’s interested in backend development. The container-world, infrastructure and devops is a little confusing…after all Alice just wants to prototype an enpoint to upload a video, ML to detect what objects are int, compress it, archive it and return a storage url. Alice discovered Storyscript. She was able to describe her business-logic in simple terms and get the job done in minutes because the tooling helped auto-complete, the services were ready-to-use like libraries and no-plumbing deployments.

(not real code, just concepts to illustrate the idea)
```
when http server listen method:"post" path:"/upload" as req
    video = req.files["upload"]
    topics = machinebox/videobox detect input:video
    res = psql insert table:"videos" data:{..stuff from above..}
    video = someOpenSourceCompressor compress video:video
    storage put content:video dest:"/{res['uuid']}.mp4"
    req write content:"/{res['uuid']}.mp4"
```

Alice had a JSFiddle-like way to rapidly prototype this but in a production-grade environment. Like Glitch, but open source and polyglot. But Zapier-like services are native libraries in our language.

In Hilltop I use blocks (order not important) to define application objects based on predefined object types. Each object definition has predefined typed attributes (including one for the object's value if it's a data-type object) which vary according to object type. The programmer assigns constant values or spreadsheet-like formulas to each attribute. As in a spreadsheet, the formulas are automatically recalculated when the value of any dependency changes. Up to this point it's FRP and declarative.

However, each attribute has an optional sub-attribute that can include imperative code for determining the parent attribute's value. Thus, for example, if defining an attribute's value would require a nested formula, the programmer can instead express it imperatively (e.g., with intermediate local variables, If statements, etc.) within the sub-attribute. This code is reactive and is evaluated whenever the value of any of its dependencies changes. Each attribute also has an optional "watcher" sub-attribute that can include imperative code that is evaluated whenever the value of the parent attribute changes. This code is not reactive and can do whatever the programmer wants, including changing the values of other attributes of its parent object or of other objects.

OK, but what pain would be caused by enforcing declarative only, no imperative feature?

Hi all, long time lurker here. I love this community; it has so much valuable content and discussions; it also gave us confidence that we were moving in the right direction. As we are getting ready to launch what we (<https://bitspark.de/>) have been working on, I want to introduce our company Bitspark. We started working on a VPL long before we knew that this community existed and where navie enough to not even search for prior art - doh’. We started from an observation we made as everyday programmers. There so much repetition, and you need to be highly trained to be a software engineer. We asked our selves what the world _after Programming_ would look like. That was in early 2018. Since then, we created a VPL runtime (<https://bitspark.de/slang/>) in Golang, making heavy use of Green Threads (each node in the tree is a green thread all connected via channels). That gave us quite the performance out of the box (<https://bitspark.de/blog/benchmarking-slang>). We have had our ups and downs and doubts and in the process, went through three different (editor) UIs. And then, in early 2019, we decided we need to build a platform around the runtime and the UI to make it easier to access and run/manage.

And that is what we (soft) launch in early November.
Why am I telling you this? We are looking for like-minded people willing to take the platform for a test drive and provide valuable feedback or provide feedback in any form or shape. (<https://bitspark.de/#private-beta>)
Yup, that's it! And that's how the Object Network and Onex work.
Yup, that's it! And that's how the Object Network and Onex work.

I have an interesting struggle. The platform I'm working on is in an early stage so I would prefer to spend most of my time working on the product (write code). On the other hand, the platform has very few users, so really I should focus on spreading the word and getting users. But obviously the two are in lockstep, the better the product the easier it is to get users.

I would really love to find an evangelist but I have no idea how. I'm not sure that's even possible / productive, as obviously it is me who knows most about the product.

How do you solve this?

How do you solve this?
I’d start with asking your very few users, if they, or one of them, would like to be more involved.

If you don’t know your users well enough to have such power users in mind, start with reaching out, offer an AMA or invite them to a Zoom call where they can offer you feedback and you can learn what they like and don’t like. Then ask for testimonials. Pretty quickly some people should stand out as asking interesting questions or having suggestions that align with your roadmap.

Laser cutters don't work well because the pumpkin doesn't have a flat surface, and the laser is only focussed over a short interval. Also, the entire pumpkin needs to be inside the machine for laser safety. But CNC mills work just fine. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3l8LYZPv44I>

Do you have a deadline, fixed runway, etc.?

I try to alternate between open and closed phases. But it takes time.

Do you have a deadline, fixed runway, etc.?

I try to alternate between open and closed phases. But it takes time.
The deadline is long enough to not matter yet (years)
The deadline is long enough to not matter yet (years)
what do you mean by open/closed?
what do you mean by open/closed?

The terms are from <https://www.amazon.com/Creativity-Flow-Psychology-Discovery-Invention/dp/0062283251>

Open = extroverted, talking about your work, listening to feedback, etc.
Closed = heads down, building, etc.

If your deadline is long enough, I recommend not trying explicitly to find an evangelist (or any kind of collaborator). Just talk to everyone you can. When someone keeps coming back, then collaboration will be much more organic.
